prompt: Describe a dinner party you would host for five interesting guests (fictional, historical, or alive). Who would they be and what would you serve?
response:
Host: me, in the small stone cottage I hope to own someday, somewhere temperate where the air smells of thyme and the sea. One long oak table outside under a fig tree strung with paper lanterns. No phones, no timepieces—just the sky dimming through indigo and the first bats flickering overhead.

Guest list (five chairs, plus mine)

1. Ursula K. Le Guin – to keep us honest about the power and limits of words.  
2. Leonardo da Vinci – to sketch the wineglasses when he thinks no one is looking.  
3. Maya Angelou – to turn every anecdote into a canticle.  
4. Anthony Bourdain – to curse beautifully and refill everyone’s glass.  
5. The alien linguist from Ted Chiang’s “Story of Your Life” – to remind us that grammar can bend time.

I would set the sixth chair for myself at the foot, so I can see all their faces when the candles gutter.

Menu – everything served family-style, platters passed clockwise so conversations collide.

First
Chilled cucumber–mint soup in handleless celadon cups, no spoons—sip, don’t stir.  
A riff on Sardinian pane carasau: wafer-thin sheets of durum cracker still warm, brushed with olive oil that has a single slice of truffle bobbing in the bottle like a tiny dark moon.

Second
Grilled mackerel that was swimming that morning, stuffed only with lemon verbena stems. Its skin blistered until it crackles like thin glass.  
Side bowl of heirloom tomatoes the size of gumballs, raw, with nothing but coarse salt and their own hot-from-the-sun perfume.

Third
A cassoulet in which duck confit shares real estate with beluga lentils and fresh coco beans, the crust broken tableside so the steam rolls up like a ghost that wants to be listened to.

Fourth
Cheese that smells of barns and wet slate: Époisses, its lid just lifted so the center sighs. With it, a spoonful of dark honeycomb still holding a single stunned bee.

Fifth
Dessert pretending to be humble: burnt-butter apricot tart, the fruit so ripe it has started to ferment in its own skin. One scoop of crème fraîche with a whisper of cardamom.

Drinks
A mineral-white Assyrtiko that tastes like licking stones struck by lightning.  
A Côtes-du-Rhône that Bourdain and da Vinci argue over—Tony praising its punk-rock garrigue, Leonardo insisting it needs another ten years and a different shaped glass.  
For the linguist, a tisane of cedar and star anise that smells of libraries in winter.  
Toward midnight, small thimbles of mez distilled from wild mountain apples; when the glasses are empty we smash them into the fire because the
